title: Pay, Stay, or Delay? How to Settle a Run
abstract: The classic view assumes banks prioritize immediate repayment by selling assets until default. We endogenize run frequency and study how general settlement rules trade off liquidity provision net of fire sale losses against induced run incentives. Panic runs are eliminated when all illiquid assets are sold under orderly resolution, but liquidity provision in a run is minimal. When suspension after some fire sales is followed by immediate liquidation, run frequency falls then rises in suspension delay. Thus, optimal suspension may require some sale of illiquid assets, in contrast to MMF norms. Ex post discretion induces excessive liquidation and more frequent runs. (JELD8, G21)

author.Enrico Perotti*: University of Amsterdam, the Netherlands & CEPR

article.1 The Basic Setup:
JEL D8 - Information, Knowledge, and Uncertainty G21 - Banks; Depository Institutions; Micro Finance Institutions; Mortgages Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

In the wake of the 2008 financial crisis, stricter rules were put in place to make financial intermediaries more resilient to runs. While the FDIC took over resolution powers to address runs on systemic nonbank intermediaries, the SEC and ESMA reformed norms for money market funds (MMFs) by introducing redemption gates and fees once redemptions force liquid reserves below some threshold. The COVID-19 crisis represented a major test for the new norms. Heavy redemptions (especially on funds subject to gates) were not met by suspension of repayment, as funds chose to sell even illiquid assets to avoid triggering a suspension of redemptions.

This paper seeks a proper assessment of settlement rules in a run on the induced run incentives, the frequency of panic runs, and overall welfare. We show how different rules trade off liquidity provision net of fire sale losses and induced run frequency, and study the challenges facing an effective implementation. Our contribution builds on the core function of banking as liquidity insurance. As outlined inDiamond and Dybvig (1983), liquidity risk sharing may be achieved by demandable debt under sequential service, where all requests for repayment are honored unconditionally until assets are exhausted. However, demandable debt may also induce fears of dilution, resulting in a panic-based run equilibrium even on a solvent bank. We follow the global-games approach adopted byGoldstein and Pauzner (2005)to resolve the ambiguity of multiple equilibria. This approach enabled them to assess the probability of a bank run and overall welfare, taking sequential service as the settlement rule in a run.

Our framework takes the next step of examining how a broader class of settlement rules affects the chance of panic-based runs and overall welfare. We are able to endogenize the chance of panic runs in closed form under a more tractable modeling of liquidity preferences. Agents with linear utility in consumption may require interim liquidity to satisfy some immediate spending needs; if unable to withdraw the required funds, they face a sizable but finite cost. Such preferences create risk-sharing benefits as inDiamond and Dybvig (1983), satisfied by intermediaries under a normalized early repayment. This combination allows us to concentrate on optimal settlement rules in a global-games framework.1

We analyse settlement rules that vary on the timing of suspension of repayment and on the timing of subsequent asset liquidation and payout. Delaying a suspension after liquid reserves are used up requires selling illiquid assets immediately. Once a stay is imposed, remaining illiquid assets may be liquidated early (SL) or allowed to mature (SM). Thus, classic sequential service (SS) is a special case when no suspension is imposed until all assets are used up. In the case of a SL rule, the timing of suspension does not affect asset value recovery since remaining assets are immediately liquidated and distributed after suspension. The optimal rule trades off liquidity provision net of asset value loss in a run against induced run incentives.

When assets are sold under orderly liquidation after a stay (SM rule), all panic-driven runs vanish as long as no illiquid assets are sold before a stay. Intuitively, under this norm all those who do not get paid before suspension (and thus also those who did not run) share the higher value of illiquid assets at maturity. This eliminates the risk of dilution and ensures that rolling over is beneficial as long as fundamentals are good. However, the lower chance of runs comes at the cost of very low liquidity provision upon a run. Under the SM rule, run frequency is always increasing in the delay to suspend payments after liquid assets are exhausted.

In contrast, when assets are sold and paid out after a suspension (under a SL rule), we show that run frequency first decreases then rises as suspension is delayed. Intuitively, delaying a stay at first increases the chance of satisfying all withdrawals, while those who roll over benefit from sharing the higher value of remaining assets at maturity. The positive effect on rollover incentives decreases when more assets are sold at a discount, as fears of dilution rise (strategic complementarity). Ultimately, further delay leads to more runs, which are maximized under the extreme case of sequential service (SS rule). This key result explains why the optimal suspension timing under SL allows some sales of illiquid assets.

We then compare the SL and SM redemption rules. The optimal suspension delay of SL trades off lower run probability (anticipating suspension) against liquidity provision upon a run (delaying suspension), while that of SM also takes into account the asset value loss (or gain) from liquidity provision upon a run. When the discount on early liquidation is low, more liquidity provision in a run is value enhancing, so SL is welfare maximizing. When instead the discount is high, SM maximizes welfare since SL yields expected value loss conditional on a run.

An important contribution of our analysis is that the optimal timing of suspension under a SL rule comesaftersome illiquid assets are sold. This is in contrast with recent regulatory reforms that introduced redemption gates for some shadow bank intermediaries (MMFs)beforeall liquid reserves have been used. The COVID-19 shock showed some limits of this approach, as hardly any MMF fund in Europe or the United States suspended payments. Some even preferred sales of illiquid assets at a discount before using reserves to avoid triggering a gate. Our result may thus justify recent SEC and ESMA reform proposals, moving away from gating for MMFs before all liquid reserves are used up. Indeed,Li et al. (2021)find that the fear of a suspension before liquidity is exhausted may have encouraged runs on MMFs in March 2020.

Theex anteoptimal rules take fully into consideration their effect on the chance of panic runs, but actual implementation of settlement rules upon a run depends on the degree of commitment. As also shown byEnnis and Keister (2009)in the classic Diamond-Dybvig framework, ex post discretionary responses once a run is underway leads to excess liquidation. Their setup however does not allow to compute the impact of discretion on the probability of a panic run. Our closed-form solution for endogenous run probability enables to assess how ex post discretion affects run incentives, run frequency, and thus ex ante welfare. Intuitively, when there is no commitment to a prespecified timing of suspension and liquidation policy, once a run is underway the social planner will optimize ex post welfare while neglecting the impact on run incentives. We are able to show that the delay before suspension and the chance of runs is higher in equilibrium without commitment, consistent with the insight byEnnis and Keister (2009). The social planner has stronger incentives to delay suspension ex post than ex ante, and agents anticipating this discretionary choice will run more often.

The Dodd-Frank Act of 2010 gave authorities discretion to trigger the Orderly Liquidation Authority (OLA), which transfers the resolution of nonbank financial institutions (such as large investment banks) to FDIC and converts some debt of uninsured creditors into equity. This bail-in arrangement resembles a suspension of repayments followed by loss sharing among unpaid uninsured creditors, with remaining assets sold under orderly resolution. Our model suggests that the triggering of OLA may be inefficiently late. This adds to the insight byWalther and White (2020), who show that discretion gives the regulators incentives to inefficiently take weaker bail-in policies to avoid sending bad news and triggering bank runs by short-term creditors.

Our model further points out that suspension followed by orderly resolution would be imposed under discretion when assets face a significant early liquidation discount. This is in line with suspensions that have been widely used in the case of highly illiquid asset markets. A major loss of confidence in 2022 led to huge outflows of deposited digital assets from many crypto intermediaries. In a context of extreme illiquidity, many intermediaries chose to suspend withdrawals. Ultimately, their bankruptcy (as in the case of the major crypto exchange FTX) will be resolved by orderly sales of remaining assets.

Our paper is related to the literature that investigates the effect of contracts and settlement rules under full commitment on bank fragility and welfare, studied in a setting in which there is no run probability (Green and Lin 2003;Andolfatto et al. 2017;Capponi, Glasserman, and Weber 2020) or the run probability is exogenous (Peck and Shell 2003,2010). A second strand studies the effect of contracts and regulations under full commitment on the run probability and welfare taking settlement rules as exogenous (Goldstein and Pauzner 2005;Allen et al. 2018;Dávila, and Goldstein 2023;Kashyap et al. 2020). A third strand studies the effect of lack of commitment on bank fragility in a framework with no run probability and welfare considerations (Ennis and Keister 2009), or with welfare implications under an exogenous run probability (Ennis and Keister 2010;Keister 2016). A recent paper on the effect of timing of suspension on the probability of bank runs offers consistent results, but it does not address welfare implications and the role of lack of commitment (Schilling 2023). Ultimately, our approach provides a role for intermediaries as liquidity providers and offers a tractable analysis of how settlement rules and commitment ability affect the run probability and ex ante welfare.

Proposition 2 says that, when fundamentals are observable, only two extreme settlement rules are necessary for a state-contingent implementation of the optimal mechanism via a unique equilibrium (strong implementation): full liquidation (⁠m=k+(1−k)ℓ⁠) and no sale of illiquid assets (m=k). Yet, the potential inability to assign different settlement rules contingent on fundamentals makes it unclear which suspension and liquidation policies are preferred ex ante. For example, ifm=kis the settlement rule chosen for allθθ∈[0,1]⁠, it brings the advantage of avoiding panic-based runs whenθθ>r−1⁠. Yet, when fundamentals are poor (⁠θθ<r−1⁠) Proposition 1 says that it only provides the best outcomes forθθ∈(θ˜,r−1)⁠. Forθθ∈(r−1ℓ,θ˜)⁠, it suboptimally prioritises asset value by allowing illiquid assets to mature. Forθθ≤r−1ℓ⁠, illiquid assets are inefficiently allowed to mature and liquidity provision is suboptimal. Furthermore, the region of best outcomesθθ∈(θ˜,r−1)is decreasing inℓ⁠, which suggests that allowing some illiquid assets to be liquidated early (m>k) may be welfare-improving whenℓis sufficiently high.

However, suspension after some illiquid assets are sold gives rise to multiple equilibria with different outcomes whenθθr>1(weak implementation). The costs of panic-based runs may surpass the benefits of better liquidity provision forθθ<θ˜and higher asset value forθθ≤r−1ℓ⁠. In addition, while sequential service (⁠m=k+(1−k)ℓ⁠) provides the maximum liquidity provision forθθ<θ˜and highest asset value forθθ≤r−1ℓ⁠, it also yields the widest range of potential panic-based runs:θθ∈(r−1,θ¯]⁠. Therefore, an interior suspensionm∈(k,k+(1−k)ℓ)may be a better solution depending on the chance of a panic-based run, as its panic-based region is smaller:θθ∈(θ^(m),θ¯]⊂(r−1,θ¯]⁠.

These results indicate that the induced likelihood of a panic-based run can be crucial for the optimal timing of suspension. We address this issue in a realistic setting with incomplete information about fundamentals.
article.3 Incomplete Information about Fundament:
Agents pool their assets via a financial intermediary int= 0 and have the right to demand an early repayment of one unit int= 1. Agents that demand early repayment are randomly lined up. We followEnnis and Keister (2009)and assume that the amount of early repayments that have already been made at any point is observable, but the measure of agents whose demands for early withdrawals have not yet been satisfied is unobservable. Thus, settlement rules can be contingent on the amount that has been repaid, but not on the overall demand for early repayments. Agents who choose to wait untilt= 2 equally share the remaining assets along with those whose demands for early withdrawals are not satisfied. Asset fundamentals are unknown int= 1, and each agent receives a private noisy signal on the stateθgiven by

θ σ η x i = θ + σ η i , (11)

whereσσ>0is arbitrarily small andηηi∼U[−12,12]are independently distributed across agents.

There is a social planner that chooses a settlement rule in order to maximize welfare. In an environment where settlement rules cannot be contingent on the stateθ, the run frequency induced by each rule is a key determinant of overall welfare. We focus on settlement rules that determine the timing of suspension of early repayments and the liquidation policy upon suspension. Formally, a settlement rule (m,f) is defined as follows: (a) early repayments are suspended after an amountαm∈[α_,mmax]is paid out, whereααα_∈(α,k)andmmax≡k+(1−k)ℓis the maximum amount that can be paid whenθθ≤θ¯⁠;4(b) after suspension, remaining illiquid assets are liquidated early (f= 1) or allowed to mature (f= 0) and resources are equally shared among unpaid agents. Sequential service (SS) is the especial case whenm=mmax⁠.

Our analysis proceeds in two steps. In Section 3.1, we consider the situation in which the social planner is committed to implementing the settlement rule that maximizes the ex ante welfare int= 0. In Section 3.1.2, we will consider the case in which the social planner cannot commit to follow the settlement rules established int= 0. On the contrary, the social planner has discretion to choose the settlement rule that maximizes ex post welfare in the event of a run int= 1.

3.1 Optimal settlement rules with commitment

In Section 3.1.1, we derive the probability of a panic-based run and characterizing the welfare-maximizing timing of suspension based on the settlement rule SLα(m∈[α_,mmax],f=1)⁠, which liquidates remaining illiquid assets soon after suspension. In Section 3.1.2, we conduct a similar analysis based on the settlement rule SMα(m∈[α_,mmax],f=0)⁠, which allows remaining illiquid assets to mature after suspension. Finally, in Section 3.1.3, we characterize the welfare-maximizing settlement rule taking as given the optimal timing of suspension in SL and SM.

As the intermediary is still using liquid reserves, the first effect of more agents repaid one unit before suspension is a higher expected final payout if the threshold type chooses to roll over and there is no suspension. The second effect is a reduction in the threshold type’s expected chance of a suspension. The third effect is a lower expected net rollover payoff in the case of suspension. According to Proposition 4, the overall impact is positive. The intuition is simple: while a suspension delay reduces the net rollover payoff upon suspension, this effect is more than offset as the threshold type becomes more confident that suspension will not be imposed and shifts probability from negative to positive net rollover payoffs.

In comparison to the benchmark under observable fundamentals, our findings highlight the importance of the timing of suspension and liquidation policy to deal with the inability to design precise state-contingent settlement rules when fundamentals are unobservable. It was clear from Proposition 2 that state-contingent rules allows for an optimal mechanism that eliminates panic-based runs. Without a precise contingency, early liquidation of illiquid assets creates dilution risk and the possibility of panic-based runs. Thus, whether early liquidation of illiquid assets can be welfare improving depends on the frequency of panic-based runs. Our global-games framework allows us to pin down the probability of panic-based runs and characterize the welfare-maximizing settlement rules under an endogenous probability of induced panic-based runs.

3.2 Optimal settlement rules without commitment.

We now examine the potential inefficiency brought about by the lack of commitment to pre-established settlement rules int= 0. In this environment, the social planner has discretion to design the settlement rules int= 1 to maximize the ex post welfare once a run is underway. article.4 Conclusion:
We study how run settlement rules affect the chance of runs on financial intermediaries and define the welfare-maximizing settlement in terms of timing of suspension and timing of asset sale and payout upon a run. A key contribution arises from deriving the chance of panic-based runs induced under both full commitment and ex post discretion. This result can be obtained under a less extreme form of liquidity preferences than in the classic contribution byDiamond and Dybvig (1983).

Run frequency is minimized when illiquid assets are allowed to mature upon suspension and a stay is imposed as soon as liquid reserves are used up, since in this case there is no dilution externality and strategic complementarity is eliminated. Run frequency increases as the suspension is delayed, so the optimal timing of suspension balances the benefit of increased liquidity provision net of value loss against the increase in the likelihood of panic-based runs. When illiquid assets are liquidated immediately upon suspension, we show that the chance of a run first decreases then rises. In this case, because the timing of suspension does not affect asset value recovery, the optimal timing of suspension trades off liquidity provision in a run and the frequency of panic runs. This insight on the chance of runs allows us to conclude that in general it is optimal to impose a stay only after some sales of illiquid assets. Classic sequential service is then a corner solution, and can be proven to be optimal when asset liquidity is quite high.

Next, we study the ex post choice under discretion, when the social planner no longer internalizes the effect on the run probability. In this case, the equilibrium ex post choices result in welfare-reducing excessive liquidity provision relative to the ex ante optimum, leading to a rise in panic-driven runs as agents anticipate a larger payout before a suspension is imposed.

Our results are relevant for the debate on measures adopted by regulators after the 2008 financial crisis, as they suggest that even a social planner may choose ex post for excessive liquidation. While a regulator with a long-term horizon may consistently seek to suspend optimally to affect expectations and reduce run incentives, concerns over possible contagion effects may still lead to excessive delay to impose a stay. As shown byWalther and White (2020), regulatory discretion will produce incentives to delay implementing bail-in policies to avoid revealing adverse information and triggering further bank runs.

Our results show that for intermediaries holding fairly illiquid assets (such as commercial banks) an early suspension rule may be optimal to avoid massive asset losses. In practice, banks are closed down before all assets are sold to meet repayments, as it became evident after the Lehman Brothers default occurred while the entity still held large amount of illiquid assets. Strict sequential service is optimal for intermediaries with highly liquid assets (narrow banks), such as Government MMFs. Indeed, suspension (gates) and redemption fees do not apply to Government MMFs. For the intermediate case represented by funds invested in less liquid assets (such as Prime MMFs), we show that imposing a suspension before at least some illiquid assets are sold is not credible under discretion. This may help explain why MMFs exposed to heavy withdrawals upon the COVID shock of March 2020 sought to avoid triggering gates by selling illiquid assets while retaining enough reserves. In response to this experience the SEC and ESMA have called for larger liquid buffers and removing gating in favor of mandatory redemption charges, to be automatically triggered upon large withdrawals. These proposals have been now implemented in the US.

While our framing enables a tractable analysis of welfare-maximizing suspension rules, more work is needed to understand the effect of redemption fees.5More complex preferences produce a less tractable framing under a global game approach (as pointed byEnnis and Keister 2009). Pursuing this goal is an important direction for future research.
